_source_of_truth_note: This file is the canonical mineral spec for all three runtimes. vugg.py is the dev/test harness (where pytest runs). index.html is the shipped product (GitHub Pages serves repo root; runtime fetches data/minerals.json and overwrites the inline MINERAL_SPEC_FALLBACK). agent-api/vugg-agent.js is intentionally kept simple and reads this file via require(). Cross-runtime drift is caught by tools/sync-spec.js.

_schema.max_nucleation_count: Maximum number of crystals of this species that can ever nucleate in a single run. Counted across the whole simulation (active + dissolved). Keeps common minerals like wulfenite from producing microcrystalline swarms that get immediately swallowed by other phases.
_schema.max_size_cm: Hard cap on crystal size — 2x world record per research/mineral-accuracy-audit.md. Growth halts when reached; this is the fix for the 321248% bug.
_schema.growth_rate_mult: Relative growth rate, calcite=1.0 baseline. Multiply the runtime base rate.
_schema.T_range_C: [min, max] temperature window for growth (C). null,null = unbounded.
_schema.pH_dissolution_below: pH below which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.pH_dissolution_above: pH above which mineral dissolves. null if stable.
_schema.required_ingredients: {species: min_ppm}. All must be present for growth.
_schema.thermal_decomp_C: Temperature above which mineral breaks down (C).
_schema.fluorescence: {activator, threshold_ppm, color, quencher} or null if non-fluorescent.
_schema.twin_laws: [{name, miller_indices, trigger, probability}] or [] if none known.
_schema.acid_dissolution: {pH_threshold, reaction, products} or null if acid-stable.
_schema.habit_variants: Array of habit objects. Each has: name, wall_spread (0-1, lateral spread on wall), void_reach (0-1, projection into vug), vector (projecting|coating|tabular|equant|dendritic), trigger (conditions that favor this habit). Growth engine selects habit based on σ, T, and space.
_schema.class: Mineralogical class for filtering/library UI: sulfide | carbonate | oxide | silicate | sulfate | phosphate | hydroxide | halide | arsenate | molybdate | tungstate | native.
_schema.description: 2-3 sentence geological description for library mode. Learning-audience voice; not flavor text.
_schema.scenarios: Array of scenario IDs (from SCENARIOS registry) in which this mineral can nucleate. Empirically verified with seed 42.
_schema.narrate_function: Name of dedicated _narrate_* function. null = inline flavor, needs refactor.
_schema.runtimes_present: Files where growth engine currently exists.
_schema.class_color: Hex color for topographic map visualization. Derived from mineral class — all classes of the same type share one hue on a 12-color wheel (30° spacing). Wall/matrix = #D2691E (amber).
_schema.test_cases: Optional array of self-validating test cases for the supersaturation gate. Each case: {name, fluid (partial FluidChemistry), T_C, [pressure_kbar], expects: {sigma: '>1.0' | '==0' | '>=0.5' | '<0.3'}, rationale}. Empty array = no declared cases (legacy minerals). New minerals should declare 2-3 cases minimum: positive gate hit, missing-ingredient block, and (if applicable) a mechanic-specific block. The generic test runner that exercises these cases is item 6 of proposals/TASK-BRIEF-DATA-AS-TRUTH.md (not yet shipped). See proposals/vugg-mineral-template.md §15 for the schema and tools/new-mineral.py for the scaffold seed.

_audit_summary._note: Post-phase-2 state. The _initial_audit block below is frozen at phase-1 start for historical reference — do not edit. +topaz (MG-B), +tourmaline/beryl/spodumene (MG-A), +anglesite/cerussite/pyromorphite/vanadinite (Pb oxidation), +bornite/chalcocite/covellite (Cu sulfide enrichment), +cuprite/azurite/native_copper (Cu oxide-native), +magnetite/lepidocrocite/stibnite/bismuthinite/native_bismuth/clinobisvanite (Fe/Sb/Bi suite), +chrysocolla (Bisbee), +marcasite (round-2: pH<5 pyrite dimorph), +wurtzite (round-2: T>95 sphalerite dimorph), +erythrite/annabergite (round-2: paired vivianite-group arsenates), +tetrahedrite/tennantite (round-2: paired fahlore Sb/As sulfosalts), +apophyllite (round-2: zeolite-facies silicate, hematite-phantom 'bloody apophyllite' habit, deccan_zeolite scenario added), +aragonite (round-3: orthorhombic CaCO3, Mg/Ca-driven polymorph competition with calcite per Folk 1974/Morse 1997), +rhodochrosite (round-3: Mn carbonate, biggest collection-weight miss at 35 specimens), +siderite (round-3: Fe carbonate, oxidation breakdown to goethite is the diagenetic pseudomorph mechanism), +dolomite (round-3: Ca-Mg ordered carbonate, completes the carbonate quartet calcite/aragonite/rhodochrosite/siderite/dolomite).

calcite.description: Retrograde solubility makes calcite the giant-maker of vugs: hotter fluid holds less Ca+CO₃, so heating precipitates calcite. Fizzes in acid. Mn²⁺ activates orange fluorescence; Fe²⁺ quenches it — the boundary between glowing and dark zones records a fluid-chemistry shift.
calcite.audit_status: ok — most complete implementation, use as template (round-3 update: Mg-poisoning factor added so calcite yields to aragonite when Mg/Ca > ~2)

habit_variants.name: acicular_needle
habit_variants.trigger: high σ — long thin needles in radiating sprays

habit_variants.name: flos_ferri
habit_variants.trigger: Fe-rich (Fe > 30 ppm) and moderate σ — dendritic 'iron flower' coral, the Eisenerz Austrian variety

aragonite.description: CaCO₃ in the orthorhombic crystal system — the metastable polymorph of calcite. Same composition, different structure, kinetically favored over thermodynamically-stable calcite when (Folk 1974, Morse 1997, Sun 2015): Mg/Ca > ~1.5 (dominant control — Mg poisons calcite growth steps but is excluded from aragonite's orthorhombic structure); T > ~50°C in low-Mg waters; supersaturation Ω > ~10 (Ostwald step rule); or trace Sr/Pb/Ba present (these cations match Ca²⁺ in aragonite's 9-fold coordination but not calcite's 6-fold). At surface pressure aragonite is metastable — over geologic time it converts to calcite (half-life ~10⁵ yr at 25°C, ~10³ yr at 80°C, faster in hot wet systems).

trace_ingredients.Sr: scavenged into the lattice (aragonite hosts Sr that calcite can't accommodate)
trace_ingredients.Pb: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Ba: scavenged into the lattice (same reason as Sr)
trace_ingredients.Mg: EXCLUDED — Mg's smaller ionic radius doesn't fit aragonite's 9-fold Ca site
trace_ingredients.Mn: minor incorporation (less than calcite, which has the right coordination)
trace_ingredients.Fe: Fe-rich fluids produce the dendritic 'flos ferri' habit

aragonite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic CaCO3 -> trigonal calcite (polymorphic conversion before calcination at ~840°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaCO3 + 2H+ -> Ca2+ + H2O + CO2 (same as calcite — fizzes)

polymorphic_conversion.trigger: T > 100°C and aragonite supersaturation drops below 0.8 (kinetic favorability lost)
polymorphic_conversion.half_life_note: Bischoff & Fyfe 1968 — solution-mediated, ~10³ yr at 80°C in water-bearing systems, ~10⁵ yr at 25°C, much longer in dry/closed systems. Pleistocene reef aragonite commonly survives; pre-Pleistocene rarely does.

aragonite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: orthorhombic CaCO3 polymorph, Mg/Ca dominant control per Folk 1974/Morse 1997, polymorphic conversion to calcite at T>100°C, four habits including iconic cyclic twin and flos ferri). Scenarios array empty until SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT brief runs — none of the existing scenarios have realistic enough Mg/Ca (>1.5) to nucleate aragonite at seed 42; existing scenarios use realistic low-Mg brines that correctly favor calcite. Aragonite gate is verified working with manually elevated Mg.

dolomite.description: CaMg(CO₃)₂ — the ordered double carbonate, with Ca and Mg in alternating cation layers (R3̄ space group, distinct from calcite's R3̄c). Rarely forms at surface temperature ('dolomite problem' in geology — modern oceans should but don't precipitate it for kinetic reasons). Above 50°C with both Ca and Mg present in roughly comparable amounts (Mg/Ca ratio 0.3-5, optimum 1:1), dolomite competes with calcite (low Mg) and theoretical magnesite (no Ca, not modeled). The host rock of MVT deposits and a major sedimentary carbonate. Saddle-shaped curved rhombohedra are the diagnostic habit — the lattice strain from cation ordering shows in the surface geometry.
dolomite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 -> CaO + MgO + 2 CO2 (decarbonation in two stages: MgCO3 first ~500°C, CaCO3 at ~840°C)

acid_dissolution.reaction: CaMg(CO3)2 + 4H+ -> Ca2+ + Mg2+ + 2 H2O + 2 CO2 (slower than calcite — Mg slows the kinetics)

nucleation_preferences.note: Calcite substrate is the classic dolomitization texture — Mg-bearing fluid replaces earlier calcite as Mg/Ca ratio climbs. Direct vug-wall nucleation also common in Mg-rich brines.

dolomite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: ordered Ca-Mg carbonate, R3̄ space group, three habits including diagnostic saddle rhombs, Mg/Ca ratio gate 0.3-5 with sweet-spot bonus near 1:1). Empirically verified in reactive_wall at seed 42 (initial Mg bumped 80→100 within Sverjensky 1981 limestone-brine range). KIM 2023 KINETICS: per Kim, Sun et al. (Science 382:915, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690), dolomite growth requires cyclic dissolution-precipitation. Implementation tracks crystal.phantom_count as cycle counter; growth rate scales by f_ord = 1 - exp(-N/50), gating from 30% (no cycling) to 100% (well-cycled). Steady-state runs produce disordered Mg-calcite intermediate, not true ordered dolomite — matches the geological observation that most natural settings produce disordered HMC, with true dolomite requiring oscillatory environments (sabkhas, microbial mats, evaporitic basins).

kim_2023_kinetics.citation: Kim J, Kimura Y, Putnis CV, Putnis A, Lee MR, Sun W (2023) Science 382:915-920, doi:10.1126/science.adi3690
kim_2023_kinetics.mechanism: Disordered Ca/Mg surface layer is more soluble than ordered alternation; brief undersaturation strips disordered atoms preferentially, leaving ordered template for next growth pulse
kim_2023_kinetics.cycle_tracking: Fluid-level — VugConditions._dol_cycle_count counts full undersaturation→supersaturation crossings, updated each step in run_step. All active dolomites benefit from accumulated cycles, even those that succeed earlier dolomite seeds enclosed by other carbonates.
kim_2023_kinetics.natural_settings: evaporitic/lacustrine wet-dry cycles (Coorong, Deep Springs Lake), sabkha tidal pumping, microbial mat diurnal pH swings
kim_2023_kinetics.demo_scenario: sabkha_dolomitization (12 flood/evap cycles → ~9 fluid cycles → f_ord ~0.72 → ORDERED dolomite)

siderite.description: FeCO₃ — the iron carbonate, a calcite-group mineral (R3̄c) with Fe²⁺ in the Ca site. Tan to deep brown depending on Fe content and trace substitution (manganosiderite ↔ rhodochrosite, ankerite ↔ dolomite). Hard-gated to REDUCING conditions because Fe²⁺ must stay reduced to be soluble; rising O₂ progressively converts siderite to goethite/limonite — the diagenetic 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorph mechanism. Forms in MVT veins (accessory), sedimentary ironstone formations, hydrothermal Fe veins, and coal-seam concretions (spherosiderite).

trace_ingredients.Ca: ankerite intermediate (pale brown shift toward dolomite)

siderite.thermal_decomp_reaction: FeCO3 -> Fe3O4/FeO + CO2 (decarbonation; oxidation route at low T -> goethite)

acid_dissolution.reaction: FeCO3 + 2H+ -> Fe2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes — same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.note: The textbook diagenetic story. Rising O2 pushes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (insoluble as carbonate); the lattice collapses and Fe + CO3 move on to grow goethite or limonite elsewhere. Mechanism behind 'limonite cube after siderite' pseudomorphs.

nucleation_preferences.note: Pyrite substrate is classic — Fe-rich sulfide oxidation upstream feeds Fe²⁺ into the carbonate-saturating fluid. In MVT systems, siderite often coats earlier sphalerite + galena as the system cools and CO3 climbs.

siderite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Fe carbonate, R3̄c calcite-group structure, four habits including spherosiderite, oxidation breakdown to goethite/limonite — the diagenetic pseudomorph mechanism). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42.

rhodochrosite.description: MnCO₃ — the rosy manganese carbonate, the pink mineral of cabinet specimens. Same R3̄c calcite-group structure with Mn²⁺ replacing Ca²⁺. Forms a continuous solid solution toward calcite via the kutnohorite (CaMn carbonate) intermediate, producing the diagnostic agate-like banding when Mn/Ca ratios oscillate. Color is intrinsic (Mn²⁺ chromophore), not a trace activator. Stability requires moderate-to-reducing conditions: aggressive oxidation flips Mn²⁺ to Mn³⁺/Mn⁴⁺ and the surface converts to black manganese oxides (pyrolusite, psilomelane). Forms in epithermal Mn-bearing veins (Capillitas Argentina, Sweet Home Colorado), metamorphosed Mn sediments (N'Chwaning South Africa), and low-T carbonate replacement zones.

trace_ingredients.Ca: kutnohorite intermediate solid solution (Ca-rich pales the color; Mn-dominant gives deep raspberry-red)
trace_ingredients.Fe: darkens toward brown when Fe-rich (siderite intermediate)

rhodochrosite.thermal_decomp_reaction: MnCO3 -> MnO + CO2 (calcination, lower than calcite's 840°C)

fluorescence.note: Mn²⁺ is the intrinsic chromophore; the same ion is the activator in calcite/sphalerite. Some rhodochrosite fluoresces dull red under LW UV, but the high Mn content can be self-quenching.

acid_dissolution.reaction: MnCO3 + 2H+ -> Mn2+ + H2O + CO2 (fizzes — same as calcite)

oxidation_breakdown.reaction: MnCO3 + O2 -> Mn3+/Mn4+ oxide rind (pyrolusite/psilomelane staining) + CO2
oxidation_breakdown.note: Mn²⁺ is the cation that makes rhodochrosite pink; oxidation converts it to higher valence Mn that forms black insoluble oxides. Museum specimens require careful storage to avoid surface darkening.

nucleation_preferences.note: Goethite substrate produces the Capillitas-style stalactitic habit. Sulfide substrate (pyrite, sphalerite, galena) is the classic epithermal vein paragenesis — rhodochrosite fills space between earlier sulfides as the system cools.

rhodochrosite.audit_status: ok (round-3 add: Mn carbonate, R3̄c calcite-group structure, four habits, Ca substitution → kutnohorite intermediate color shift, oxidation breakdown to Mn-oxide rind). Empirically verified in mvt at seed 42 (initial Mn bumped 8→25 within Tri-State district brine range per Roedder 1976). The biggest collection-weight miss is now in the game (35 specimens). Future SCENARIO-CHEMISTRY-AUDIT will surface it more broadly via dedicated Capillitas / Sweet Home / N'Chwaning scenarios.

sphalerite.description: ZnS, the dominant zinc ore. Classic MVT mineral — forms at 60-200°C where metal brines meet H₂S-bearing groundwater. Iron substitution darkens the color from pale yellow (low-Fe cleiophane) through honey-brown to nearly black marmatite.

wurtzite.description: (Zn,Fe)S, the hexagonal high-temperature dimorph of sphalerite — same composition, different crystal structure. Above 95°C, cubic sphalerite gives way to hexagonal wurtzite with the distinctive hemimorphic habit (one end pointed, one end flat, due to the non-centrosymmetric P6₃mc space group). On cooling below 95°C, wurtzite converts to sphalerite — but sphalerite rarely goes back. The asymmetric dimorphism means finding wurtzite is diagnostic of rapid cooling from the hexagonal field.

trace_ingredients.Fe: substitutes for Zn, darkens color (same as sphalerite, but hotter so more Fe uptake)

acid_dissolution.reaction: (Zn,Fe)S + 2H+ → Zn2+ + H2S (slow, only in strong acid — same as sphalerite)

wurtzite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: T>95 sphalerite dimorph, hexagonal P6₃mc, hemimorphic habit, polymorphic inversion handled).

pyrite.description: FeS₂, fool's gold. Enormous T range (25-700°C), always reducing. Oxidation produces goethite/limonite plus sulfuric acid — the acid-mine-drainage reaction. Three common habits: cubic with striated faces, 12-face pyritohedral, and raspberry-shaped framboidal aggregates from sedimentary microbial sulfate reduction.

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — pyrite + O2 + H2O -> Fe2+ + SO4 + H+ (acid mine drainage)

marcasite.description: FeS₂, the orthorhombic dimorph of pyrite — same formula, different crystal structure. Acidic (pH<5), low-T (<240°C) fluids switch FeS₂ from cubic pyrite to orthorhombic marcasite. Diagnostic cockscomb and spearhead habits, paler brass than pyrite with iridescent tarnish. Metastable: converts to pyrite over geologic time or on heating, and rots in humid air to sulfuric acid and iron sulfate (the classic museum-specimen problem).
marcasite.thermal_decomp_reaction: orthorhombic FeS2 → cubic FeS2 (pyrite) — metastable inversion, not true decomposition

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative breakdown in humid air — FeS2 + O2 + H2O → FeSO4 + H2SO4 (museum rot); extreme acid dissolves directly

nucleation_preferences.note: Acid-loving FeS2 dimorph. In MVT deposits marcasite often coats earlier sphalerite/galena. Subsurface goethite/limonite pseudomorphs after marcasite are the classic Egyptian Prophecy Stones.

marcasite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: pH<5 & T<240 dimorph of pyrite, full habit set, metastable inversion handled).

uraninite.description: UO₂, the primary uranium mineral. Pitch-black, extremely dense, radioactive. Forms under strongly reducing conditions — any oxygen converts it to yellow secondary uranium minerals. Each decay turns a U atom into Pb; old uraninite accumulates radiogenic lead that eventually precipitates as nearby galena.

ferrimolybdite.description: Fe₂(MoO₄)₃·nH₂O, the no-lead branch of Mo oxidation. Canary-yellow acicular tufts to powdery crusts on molybdenite surfaces. Fast-growing, powdery, hardness ~2. Coexists with wulfenite (both draw on oxidized MoO₄²⁻) but wins the early window. Geologically more common than wulfenite; under-represented in collections because it's yellow fuzz, not display material. Type locality Climax (Colorado); abundant in any porphyry Cu-Mo oxidation zone.

arsenopyrite.description: FeAsS, the arsenic gateway mineral and the #1 gold-trapping mineral. Mesothermal primary sulfide (300-500°C, reducing); silver-white metallic prisms with diamond cross-section, deep longitudinal striations. Emits garlic odor when struck (As vapor — diagnostic). Structurally traps Au up to ~1500 ppm as 'invisible gold' (Reich et al. 2005; Cook & Chryssoulis 1990) — released to fluid on supergene oxidation, producing the orogenic Au-enrichment signature (Graeme et al. 2019 for Bisbee). Oxidation pathway: FeAsS + O₂ + H₂O → Fe³⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ + H₂SO₄ → feeds scorodite + acidifies.

trace_ingredients.Au: invisible-gold up to 1500 ppm structurally bound

arsenopyrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: FeAsS -> FeAs2 (loellingite) + S; As vapor at higher T
arsenopyrite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-UNIMPLEMENTED.md. The #1 gold-trapping mineral — Au-trapping mechanic consumes fluid Au (30% of 5%/step) and records as trace_Au on GrowthZone; supergene oxidation releases 12%/step back to fluid (feeds scorodite + native_gold supergene enrichment). Competes with grow_native_gold for fluid Au in reducing high-T scenarios.

barite.description: BaSO₄, the densest non-metallic mineral collectors will encounter at 4.5 g/cm³ (the name 'barytes' from Greek βαρύς, 'heavy'). Galena's primary gangue mineral in MVT districts (Tri-State, Cumberland, Pine Point) and abundant in hydrothermal vein systems (Cavnic, Wegscheid, Sterling Hill). Acid-resistant — even concentrated H₂SO₄ won't touch it, the basis for its industrial use as drilling-mud weighting agent. Forms a partial solid solution with celestine (BaSO₄ ↔ SrSO₄); intermediates 'celestobarite' and 'barytocelestine' are common when both Ba and Sr present in the parent fluid. Famous habits: tabular 'desert rose' (Oklahoma + Saudi Arabia), bladed Cumberland fans, cockscomb cyclic twins (Romania), honey-yellow Pb-bearing variants (Cumberland gold).
barite.thermal_decomp_reaction: BaSO4 -> BaO + SO3 (decomposition; very high T — outside normal sim range)

fluorescence.note: Most barite non-fluorescent; the Sterling Hill / Franklin (NJ) zinc-mineral co-occurrence is the only common fluorescent locality.

barite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The Ba sequestration mineral; activates the dormant fluid.Ba pool. Tri-State Ba=20 currently dormant due to scenario O2=0.0 (gap flagged in audit). Sabkha Ba=0 (correctly absent — sabkha is Sr-dominant). Real activation: porphyry post-oxidation; future MVT chemistry tweak (O2 0→0.2) would unlock Tri-State barite.

celestine.description: SrSO₄, the strontium sulfate; isostructural with barite (the two form a partial solid solution; intermediates 'celestobarite' and 'barytocelestine' common in mixed-cation fluids). The diagnostic pale celestial blue is an F-center defect color — radiation-damaged anion vacancies absorb yellow-orange light, leaving the calm sky blue that named the species. Famous occurrences: Madagascar Sakoany geodes (football-sized, 30 cm pale-blue tabular plates — world's largest), Sicilian Caltanissetta sulfur-vug fibrous habit (radiating fibers on yellow native sulfur), Lake Erie / Put-in-Bay (Crystal Cave on South Bass Island — world's largest known geode at 35 ft³). Industrial use: source of Sr for red firework colors; ⁸⁷Sr/⁸⁶Sr-isotope tracer for paleobrine geochronology (Schwartz et al. 2018).
celestine.thermal_decomp_reaction: SrSO4 -> SrO + SO3 (decomposition; high T — outside normal sim range)

jarosite.description: KFe³⁺₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆, the diagnostic acid-mine-drainage mineral. Yellow-to-ocher pseudocubic rhombs and powdery crusts; the supergene Fe-sulfate that takes over from goethite when pH drops below 4. Confirmed on Mars at Meridiani Planum by NASA's MER Opportunity Mössbauer spectrometer in 2004 (Klingelhöfer et al. 2004) — the first confirmation of past liquid water + acidic conditions on Mars. Earth localities: Rio Tinto (Spain — the Phoenician + Roman + Spanish + Rio Tinto Co. mining district that names the red-orange river), Red Mountain Pass (Colorado), Mojave acid drainage sites, every active sulfide-mine tailings pond. Above pH 4 dissolves and the released Fe³⁺ goes to goethite — the diagnostic AMD weathering succession.

trace_ingredients.Na: natrojarosite (Na replaces K) — common in salty AMD
trace_ingredients.Pb: plumbojarosite (Pb in K site) — at Pb-rich AMD localities

jarosite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KFe3(SO4)2(OH)6 -> hematite + K-sulfate + H2O (loses lattice OH)

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 4 (alkaline-side dissolution, not acid), releasing K + Fe³⁺ + SO₄²⁻; goethite-stable territory now
acid_dissolution.note: jarosite is the acid mineral; pH > 4 destabilizes it. Inverse of typical acid_dissolution semantics (same as scorodite).

jarosite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The Mars-class AMD mineral. Activates after Bingham event_oxidation (step 85) and Bisbee ev_uplift_weathering (step 65) when pH drops + O2 rises. Tsumeb pH=6.8 too high (same Tsumeb pH-gap as scorodite — flagged in audit). alunite.description: KAl₃(SO₄)₂(OH)₆, the K-Al sister of jarosite (alunite group, isostructural). The index mineral of 'advanced argillic' alteration in porphyry-Cu lithocaps and high-sulfidation epithermal Au deposits — when geologists say a porphyry has a 'lithocap', alunite + kaolinite + pyrophyllite + dickite are the diagnostic phases of the kilometers-wide acid-altered horizon above the intrusion. Type locality Marysvale (Utah), where alunite was mined as a K-fertilizer source 1915-1930s before Carlsbad potash made K cheaper to mine elsewhere. World-class epithermal-Au localities: Goldfield (Nevada), Summitville (Colorado), Tambo (Chile), Yanacocha (Peru), Nansatsu district (Japan). ⁴⁰Ar/³⁹Ar geochronology preserves the K-Ar age of the acid-sulfate hydrothermal event, critical for porphyry-system timing (Stoffregen et al. 2000).

trace_ingredients.Na: natroalunite (Na replaces K) — high-sulfidation epithermal common variant

alunite.thermal_decomp_reaction: KAl3(SO4)2(OH)6 -> corundum + K-Al-sulfate (loses lattice OH; basis for the early-1900s K-fertilizer process)

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 4 OR above 350°C, releasing K + Al³⁺ + SO₄²⁻
acid_dissolution.note: alunite is the acid mineral; pH > 4 destabilizes it. Same inverse semantics as jarosite.

pinkish.trigger: Marysvale type locality OR Fe > 20 (intermediate to jarosite, natroalunite series)

alunite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The advanced argillic alteration index mineral. Activates after Bingham event_oxidation when both pH drops AND O2 rises (porphyry lithocap simulation). Tsumeb K=10 borderline + Al=3 too low + pH=6.8 too high — won't form there. Bisbee K=80 + Al=20 + S=150 strong but needs ev_uplift_weathering for the right pH/O2 window. Closes a known modeling gap: previously the sim had no representation of advanced argillic alteration (the kilometers-wide lithocap above porphyry-Cu intrusions).

brochantite.description: Cu₄(SO₄)(OH)₆, the wet-supergene Cu sulfate. Emerald-green prismatic crystals, distinguishable from malachite by distinctly darker green and prismatic habit (vs malachite's botryoidal). The higher-pH end (pH 4-7) of the brochantite ↔ antlerite Cu-sulfate fork — acidification (pH < 3) converts brochantite to antlerite + H₂O; alkalinization (pH > 7) converts to tenorite/malachite. Atacama Desert (Chile) supergene Cu deposits — Chuquicamata, Mansa Mina, Mantos Blancos, El Tesoro — have brochantite as a major component. Bisbee, Mt Lyell (Tasmania), Cap Garonne (France), Cananea (Mexico) all have well-known specimens. Statue of Liberty's iconic green patina is largely brochantite (chloride-rich harbor air drives the SO₄/CO₃ partition toward sulfate, not malachite).

trace_ingredients.Cl: atacamite competition (Cu hydroxychloride wins when Cl > SO4)

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves below pH 3 (acidification → antlerite stable) OR above pH 7.5 (alkalinization → tenorite/malachite stable). Two-sided fork dissolution.
acid_dissolution.note: Two-sided pH fork: brochantite stable pH 3-7. Outside that window dissolves into either antlerite or tenorite/malachite cation pool.

brochantite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. antlerite.description: Cu₃(SO₄)(OH)₄, the dry-acid end of the brochantite ↔ antlerite Cu-sulfate fork (pH 1-3.5 stability). Same emerald-green color as brochantite — visually indistinguishable in hand specimen — distinguished by chemistry (Cu₃ vs Cu₄, more SO₄ per Cu unit) and acid-resistance test (antlerite dissolves in dilute HCl; brochantite resists). Type locality Antler mine (Mohave County, Arizona — Hillebrand 1889). World-class deposits at Chuquicamata (Chile) where antlerite was the dominant supergene Cu mineral mined 1920s-50s before the deeper hypogene chalcocite zone became the modern target. Coexists with brochantite + atacamite + chalcanthite at most Atacama supergene Cu deposits. The pH-fork mechanism (Pollard et al. 1992) is the most diagnostic chemistry in arid-supergene Cu mineralogy.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 3.5 (neutralization → brochantite stable). Below pH 1 → chalcanthite (extreme acid; not in sim).
acid_dissolution.note: Inverse acid_dissolution semantics: antlerite is the acid-side mineral; alkalinization destabilizes it. Same pattern as scorodite/jarosite/alunite.

antlerite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. The dry-acid Cu sulfate; lower-pH end of brochantite ↔ antlerite fork. Activates in Bisbee post-ev_uplift_weathering if pH drops below 3.5 (acidification). The brochantite ↔ antlerite reversible conversion (substrate priority on dissolving brochantite at 80%) implements the Atacama / Chuquicamata pH-cycling paragenesis.

anhydrite.description: CaSO₄, the anhydrous calcium sulfate; sister of selenite/gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O). The anhydrite-gypsum boundary is not a single line but an XY plot of T vs salinity (Hardie 1967): in pure water anhydrite stable above 60°C, but in saline brines (>100‰ NaCl-eq) anhydrite stable down to surface T due to lowered water activity. Two distinct geological occurrences: (1) high-T hydrothermal (porphyry-Cu deep-brine zones at 200-700°C — Bingham Roedder 1971); (2) low-T evaporite (Persian Gulf / Coorong sabkha + Salar de Atacama — anhydrite + halite + dolomite stratigraphy). Below 60°C in dilute fluid, anhydrite is metastable and rehydrates to gypsum/selenite. Naica's Cave of Crystals shows exactly this: an older anhydrite floor preserved from the deeper brine phase, overgrown by giant selenite blades when the cave dewatered + cooled. Industrial: cement-setting accelerator, sulfur source, plaster manufacture (anhydrite ↔ gypsum hydration cycle). Pale-lavender 'angelite' Peruvian variety is a metaphysical-stone fixture.
anhydrite.thermal_decomp_reaction: CaSO4 -> CaO + SO3 (decomposition; very high T — outside normal sim range)

acid_dissolution.reaction: rehydrates to gypsum (CaSO4·2H2O = selenite in sim) at T<60°C AND salinity<100‰; releases Ca²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ to feed selenite re-precipitation
acid_dissolution.note: Not acid-dissolution — REHYDRATION to gypsum. The anhydrite ↔ gypsum cycle is the diagnostic Naica paragenesis. Hysteresis at salinity<95‰ to avoid oscillation.

pale_lavender_angelite.trigger: Mn > 3 OR (T < 60 AND salinity > 150) — Peruvian metaphysical-stone variant

anhydrite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-SULFATES.md. Two-mode T behavior: high-T branch (>60°C) for Bingham porphyry deep brine, low-T saline branch (T<60 AND salinity>100‰) for Coorong sabkha evaporite. Sabkha activates immediately (T=25, salinity=120, S=2700, Ca=400 → sigma 7.5). Bingham init O2=0.2 below the 0.3 gate (anhydrite blocked at startup); fires after event_oxidation raises O2. Tsumeb dilute low-T (sal=2) correctly blocked. Closes the Bingham porphyry deep-brine + Coorong sabkha evaporite niches that selenite alone couldn't cover.

scorodite.description: FeAsO₄·2H₂O, the arsenic-sequestration mineral. Pseudo-octahedral pale blue-green dipyramids; the most common supergene oxidation product of arsenopyrite. Locks AsO₄³⁻ in stable crystals as long as pH stays below 5; above pH 5 it dissolves and releases AsO₄³⁻ for the higher-pH arsenate suite (erythrite, mimetite, adamite, pharmacosiderite). Type locality Freiberg, Saxony; world-class deep-blue crystals at Tsumeb (Gröbner & Becker 1973). Dehydrates to anhydrous FeAsO₄ above 160°C.

acid_dissolution.reaction: dissolves above pH 5 (alkaline-side dissolution, not acid), releasing Fe³⁺ + AsO₄³⁻
acid_dissolution.note: scorodite is the acidic-end arsenate; pH > 5 destabilizes it. Inverse of typical acid_dissolution semantics.

scorodite.audit_status: phase-2: wired in vugg.py and web/index.html. Research source: proposals/MINERALS-RESEARCH-UNIMPLEMENTED.md. tetrahedrite.description: Cu₁₂Sb₄S₁₃ — the Sb-endmember 'fahlore,' German miner's term for the steel-gray copper sulfosalts that paid in silver as much as in copper. Hydrothermal 100-400°C, optimum 200-300°C. Cubic I4̄3m space group produces diagnostic {111} tetrahedra. Paired with tennantite (As endmember) in a continuous solid solution: the Sb:As ratio of an ore fluid is recorded directly in the composition. Ag substitutes for Cu to produce the silver ore variety 'freibergite.'

trace_ingredients.Ag: freibergite variety — silver ore (up to 18% Ag)

acid_dissolution.reaction: oxidative dissolution — Cu²⁺ + Sb³⁺ released (→ secondary Sb oxides, malachite/azurite downstream)

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tennantite (As endmember). Classic porphyry-to-epithermal transition mineral — grows on earlier chalcopyrite as the fluid cools and Sb activity climbs.

tetrahedrite.audit_status: ok (round-2 add: paired with tennantite, same cubic structure, Sb endmember fahlore).

tennantite.description: Cu₁₂As₄S₁₃ — the As-endmember fahlore, paired with tetrahedrite. Same cubic I4̄3m structure; arsenic replaces antimony. Visually indistinguishable from tetrahedrite except by the cherry-red thin-fragment transmission test (tennantite transmits; tetrahedrite stays opaque). On oxidation releases Cu + AsO₄³⁻, feeding the secondary arsenate paragenesis: adamite, erythrite, annabergite, mimetite. Closes the loop between primary copper sulfosalts and downstream supergene arsenates.

cherry_red_transmission.trigger: thin edge under strong backlight — diagnostic for tennantite vs tetrahedrite

nucleation_preferences.note: Paired with tetrahedrite. Epithermal/porphyry transition mineral that feeds the entire downstream arsenate chain when it oxidizes.

Imperial topaz from Ouro Preto crystallized at ~360°C, 3.5 kbar from metamorphic hydrothermal fluids (Morteani et al. 2002). Crystals can snap cleanly along basal cleavage — pocket floors littered with cleavage fragments are diagnostic.

tourmaline.description: Complex cyclosilicate — trigonal, elongated prisms with deep vertical striations and a slightly rounded triangular cross-section. Schorl (black, Fe²⁺-dominant) crystallizes early from pegmatite melt; elbaite (colored, Li-dominant) crystallizes late from residual pocket fluid as Fe depletes and Li accumulates. Striations are growth records — each ridge is a growth pulse. Color is a fluid composition snapshot: Mn²⁺ → pink (rubellite), Cr³⁺/V³⁺ → green (verdelite), Fe²⁺+Ti → blue (indicolite), Cu²⁺ → neon Paraíba blue (extreme rarity). The Jonas mine in Minas Gerais was the world's greatest rubellite producer. The most Minas Gerais mineral there is.

acid_dissolution.reaction: extremely resistant — no practical acid dissolution

tourmaline.audit_status: new (Minas Gerais Variant A, tourmaline schorl→elbaite series). First B consumer in the sim. Not yet in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

beryl.description: Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈ — hexagonal cyclosilicate; in this simulator's post-Round-7 architecture the beryl entry represents goshenite and the generic colorless/pale beryl that fires when no chromophore trace is above threshold. Emerald (Cr/V), morganite (Mn), heliodor (Fe+oxidizing), and aquamarine (Fe reducing/moderate) are each first-class species with their own engines. Beryllium is the most incompatible common element in magmatic systems: no rock-forming mineral takes it, so Be accumulates in residual pegmatite fluid until beryl finally nucleates at high threshold. That's why beryl crystals can be enormous — by the time the first crystal fires, there's a lot of Be waiting.

trace_ingredients.Cs: red beryl / bixbite (extremely rare, not tracked here)

spodumene.description: LiAlSi₂O₆ — monoclinic pyroxene. Flattened tabular prisms that read as 'book' shape, with two cleavage directions at ~87° producing characteristic parting. Can reach 14 meters — among the longest single crystals on Earth (Etta mine, South Dakota). Lithium accumulates late in pegmatite crystallization because no early mineral takes it, so spodumene is the residual-fluid signature. Kunzite (pink-lilac) from Mn²⁺ substitution shows strong pink-orange SW fluorescence. Hiddenite (green) from Cr³⁺ is rarer. Named for George Kunz, Tiffany's mineralogist, who bought Minas Gerais specimens by the crate.

pyromorphite.description: Pb₅(PO₄)₃Cl — hexagonal apatite-group phosphate. Barrel-shaped prisms in olive-green, yellow-green, brown, or orange. Forms in supergene oxidation zones when phosphate-bearing meteoric water meets existing Pb minerals — galena, anglesite, cerussite can all be pseudomorphed. Apatite-group solid-solution end-member: pyromorphite–mimetite–vanadinite is one of mineralogy's most complete compositional series. Low toxicity (stable Pb) — pyromorphite is used in environmental remediation of Pb-contaminated soils by feeding the soil phosphate.

bornite.description: Cu₅FeS₄ — the 228°C order-disorder transition mineral. Above 228°C, Cu and Fe randomly occupy the cation sites (pseudo-cubic, high-T form); below, they order (orthorhombic). Fresh bronze-copper color on a broken surface, but the mineral tarnishes rapidly to the iridescent blue/purple/green/gold 'peacock ore' from thin-film interference on sulfide surface products. Forms both hypogene (hydrothermal, ~200–500°C) and supergene (cooler, as an enrichment mineral replacing chalcopyrite). Competes with chalcopyrite for Cu+Fe+S at different Cu:Fe ratios.

chalcocite.description: Cu₂S — 79.8% Cu by weight, one of the richest copper ores. The pseudomorph thief: replaces chalcopyrite and bornite atom-by-atom in the supergene enrichment blanket, keeping the host's outline while swapping the chemistry. Forms dark gray to black metallic crystals. Pseudo-hexagonal stellate twins (cyclic sixlings on {110}) are the collector's prize. The supergene enrichment zone is where mineable copper ore gets made — weathering concentrates Cu into a layer richer than the primary sulfide above it.

covellite.description: CuS — indigo-blue, the only common naturally blue mineral. Micaceous perfect basal cleavage (peels into flexible sheets like mica). Decomposes to chalcocite + sulfur at 507°C. The name is Latin 'covellite' (for Niccolo Covelli, who discovered the Vesuvius fumarole specimens in 1833).

azurite.description: Cu₃(CO₃)₂(OH)₂ — deep azure to midnight blue, the deepest blue in the common mineral kingdom. Forms in supergene oxidation zones where high pCO₂ groundwater meets Cu²⁺. Most azurite is born to become malachite: CO₂ escapes from solution over time and the higher-carbonate azurite converts to lower-carbonate malachite, preserving the original crystal shape as a green pseudomorph. Requires carbonate country rock (limestone host) for the high pCO₂ conditions.

native_copper.description: Cu — elemental native copper. Only forms when S²⁻ is absent (otherwise precipitates as sulfide) AND Eh is strongly reducing (otherwise oxidizes to cuprite/malachite). The Michigan Keweenaw peninsula basalt vesicles produced 500-ton masses from basalt-hosted brines. Copper-red fresh, tarnishes brown, eventually green (malachite coating). The Statue of Liberty's iconic patina IS malachite growing on native copper — the reverse of supergene ore formation, where rain and air turn metal into mineral in front of us.

native_gold.description: Au — elemental native gold, the noble metal. Two precipitation pathways the sim collapses into one supersaturation function: (1) high-T magmatic-hydrothermal Au-Cl complex destabilization at boiling/decompression (Bingham vapor-plume mechanism per Landtwing et al. 2010), and (2) low-T supergene Au-Cl reduction at the redox interface, often coupled with chalcocite enrichment (Bisbee oxidation-cap mechanism per Graeme et al. 2019). Tolerates both oxidizing AND reducing fluids because the two transport complexes (Au-Cl and Au-HS) cover both Eh regimes. Crystal habits range from octahedral {111} (slow growth, rare) through dendritic / spongy (the diagnostic supergene Au) to massive nuggets (rapid precipitation in pockets). Color goes from rich yellow at pure Au through pale yellow electrum (>10% Ag alloy) to rose-gold cuproauride affinity (Cu-rich systems like Bisbee). Essentially indestructible in surface conditions — no acid-dissolution path in the engine.

trace_ingredients.Te: Au-Te telluride competition (calaverite/sylvanite if Te + S available — future engine)

native_gold.thermal_decomp_reaction: Au melts at 1064°C; mineral itself stable at all sim T (no thermal decomposition path)

magnetite.description: Fe₃O₄ — mixed-valence Fe²⁺Fe³⁺₂O₄ spinel oxide. Black, metallic-to-submetallic, strongly magnetic (lodestone is natural permanent-magnet magnetite). Forms at the hematite-magnetite (HM) redox buffer — one of geochemistry's most important reference frames. Crossing the HM boundary transforms the entire mineral assemblage. Octahedral {111} and rhombic dodecahedral {110} are the classic habits; martite pseudomorph is magnetite after oxidation to hematite at the crystal surface. Streak: black (diagnostic against hematite's red streak).
magnetite.thermal_decomp_reaction: Fe₃O₄ oxidizes to Fe₂O₃ above 800°C in air (martite)

lepidocrocite.description: γ-FeOOH — the ruby-red hydroxide dimorph of goethite. Same formula, different crystal structure: goethite is a 3D framework (yellow-brown needles), lepidocrocite is layered (ruby-red micaceous plates). Particle size controls the hue — nanoscale plates appear pink to mauve, coarser crystals rust-red. Kinetically favored over goethite under RAPID oxidation of Fe²⁺ (e.g., pyrite weathering in situ); slow oxidation gives goethite instead. Transforms to goethite over geological time or to maghemite/hematite when heated. 'Lithium quartz' sold in rock shops is actually nanoscale lepidocrocite included in quartz.
lepidocrocite.audit_status: new (round 4). γ-FeOOH dimorph of goethite, kinetically favored in rapid oxidation. Not in agent-api/vugg-agent.js.

stibnite.description: Sb₂S₃ — orthorhombic antimony sulfide, lead-gray to steel-gray with brilliant metallic luster on fresh cleavage. One of the most visually striking minerals: sword-like elongated blades up to 60 cm at Ichinokawa (Japan). Same structure as bismuthinite (Bi₂S₃) — Sb and Bi are geochemical cousins. Low-melting (550°C), so stibnite is destroyed by minor metamorphism. Primary Sb ore. Dendritic growth when rapidly deposited; single sword-blades when slow.

bismuthinite.description: Bi₂S₃ — orthorhombic bismuth sulfide, same structure as stibnite. Lead-gray to tin-white metallic, often showing yellowish/iridescent tarnish. Forms in high-T hydrothermal veins associated with arsenopyrite, chalcopyrite, cassiterite, wolframite — the tin-tungsten-arsenic greisen suite. Temperature-driven habit switch: acicular needles at T < 350°C, stout prisms at T > 350°C. When S²⁻ runs out before Bi, switches to native bismuth.

native_bismuth.description: Bi — elemental native bismuth. Silver-white on fresh surface, iridescent oxide tarnish (yellow, blue, pink) develops rapidly. Melts at an unusually low 271.5°C — the lowest of any native metal. Forms only when sulfur runs out before bismuth does — bismuthinite uses the available S first, then residual Bi crystallizes native. The rainbow 'hoppered' Bi crystals sold in rock shops are lab-grown, not natural; natural native bismuth is typically arborescent/massive/rarely rhombohedral.

clinobisvanite.description: BiVO₄ — bright yellow to orange-yellow monoclinic bismuth vanadate. End of the bismuth oxidation sequence: bismuthinite → native bismuth → bismite/bismutite → clinobisvanite (if V is present). Microscopic — individual crystals rarely exceed 0.1 mm, so clinobisvanite appears as a powdery to micro-platy coating on matrix. Famously, BiVO₄ is a photocatalyst for water splitting — the same mineral that forms as a supergene afterthought is being engineered to make solar fuel. Folded into phosphate class_color (#13eb7f) per the 12-hue palette decision.

emerald.description: Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈ with Cr³⁺ (or V³⁺) substituting for Al — the green variety of beryl. The 'emerald paradox': Cr is ultramafic, Be is pegmatitic; these chemistries almost never coexist. Emerald forms where pegmatite fluid meets ultramafic country rock (Colombia Muzo shale-hosted, Zambia Kagem schist-hosted, Cruzeiro pegmatite-only). Top Colombian gem emerald is rarer than diamond as gem material.

Santa Maria (Pakistan Shigar) is the deep-blue benchmark; Cruzeiro and Padre Paraíso (Brazil Minas Gerais) are the volume producers. Fires when Fe ≥ 8 ppm in the Fe²⁺ state (non-oxidizing).

morganite.description: Be₃Al₂Si₆O₁₈ with Mn²⁺ — the pink-to-peach variety of beryl. Mn²⁺ sits in the Al octahedral site; natural irradiation over geologic time partially oxidizes it to Mn³⁺, producing the pink hue. Named by George F. Kunz of Tiffany & Co (1911) for J.P. Morgan. Late-stage pegmatite mineral — Mn accumulates in fluid while earlier phases (feldspar, quartz, aquamarine) crystallize. Pala District CA, Madagascar, Minas Gerais are top sources.

Volodarsk (Namibia) is the type locality for deep-yellow 'Namibian heliodor'; Urals (Russia) and Minas Gerais (Brazil) also produce.

corundum.description: Al₂O₃ — trigonal close-packed oxide. The structural parent of ruby + sapphire, and the hardness-9 abrasive mineral (emery is Al₂O₃ + magnetite). Corundum is SiO₂-undersaturated: if any significant silica is present, Al + SiO₂ drives toward the Al₂SiO₅ polymorphs (kyanite/andalusite/sillimanite) or feldspar, not corundum. The defining constraint is SiO₂ < 50 ppm — the first upper-bound gate in this simulator. Forms in amphibolite-to-granulite-facies marble contact metamorphism (Mogok-type) and in basalt-hosted xenocrysts.

trace_ingredients.Cr: trace colorless at <2 ppm (above becomes ruby)

ruby.description: Al₂O₃ + Cr³⁺ — the red chromium-bearing variety of corundum. Cr substitutes for Al at the 100-5000 ppm level in the Al octahedral site; the d-d transitions absorb blue-green and transmit red, plus fluoresce strongly red under UV (the 'ruby laser' physics). Top gem 'pigeon's blood' ruby from Mogok Burma shows medium-dark red with blue undertone from trace Fe + strong Cr fluorescence. Requires SiO₂ undersaturation (shared corundum constraint) AND Cr >= 2 ppm. Below Cr=2 the same corundum crystal would be pink sapphire or colorless.

sapphire.description: Al₂O₃ with Fe + Ti (blue), Fe alone (yellow), V (violet), or trace Cr (pink-orange padparadscha) — the non-red corundum varieties. Blue sapphire's color is the Fe²⁺-Ti⁴⁺ intervalence charge transfer (IVCT), a well-characterized gem mechanism requiring both cations at the right concentration. Kashmir 'cornflower blue' is the classic; Montana Yogo, Sri Lanka, Madagascar, Thailand/Cambodia are active sources. Shares the SiO₂ < 50 upper gate with corundum + ruby.

acanthite.description: Ag₂S — monoclinic silver sulfide and the most important silver ore on Earth at 87% Ag by weight. Lead-gray to iron-black metallic, Mohs 2-2.5, sectile (cuts like lead). The cold-storage form: above 173°C the same composition crystallizes as cubic argentite, and most acanthite in nature is paramorphic after cooled argentite, retaining the cubic external form while the lattice underneath inverts to monoclinic (Petruk et al. 1974, Canadian Mineralogist 12). Forms in low-sulfidation epithermal veins, mesothermal silver veins, and supergene enrichment zones; classic localities include Guanajuato (Mexico, pseudo-cubic crystals), Freiberg (Saxony), and Kongsberg (Norway).
acanthite.audit_status: Round 8a-1 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. Habit variants: massive_granular (low σ, dominant economic form), prismatic (mid σ), thorn (high σ), pseudo_cubic (paramorph hook — set in 8a-2 when argentite cools through 173°C). Sources: Petruk et al. 1974 (Canadian Mineralogist 12: 365-369), Hayba & Bethke 1985 (Reviews in Economic Geology 2). Activates dormant Ag pool at MVT/Sweetwater/Tsumeb/Bisbee. First Ag mineral in the sim.

argentite.description: Ag₂S — body-centered cubic silver sulfide, the high-temperature polymorph stable only above 173°C. A ghost mineral: every argentite specimen at room temperature is actually acanthite (monoclinic) wearing argentite's cubic crystal habit, because below 173°C the lattice inverts in place while the external form stays frozen — handled by the apply_paramorph_transitions hook in vugg.py. Lead-gray to black metallic, Mohs 2-2.5, sectile. Forms in the high-T zones of epithermal and mesothermal Ag-bearing veins; classic localities include Comstock Lode (Nevada), Pachuca-Real del Monte (Mexico), and Freiberg (Saxony). Source: research/research-argentite.md (boss commit f2939da), Petruk et al. 1974.
argentite.audit_status: Round 8a-2 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html. Habit variants: cubic (low-moderate σ, Comstock Lode signature), octahedral (mid σ, with {111} spinel-law penetration twins), arborescent (high σ, dendritic/wire-like). Activates dormant Ag pool only in scenarios that reach >173°C — currently radioactive_pegmatite per declared scenarios. Sources: Petruk et al. 1974 (Canadian Mineralogist 12), boss research file research/research-argentite.md.

native_silver.description: Ag — elemental silver, the only native element bright enough to make you rich just by looking at it wrong. Cubic isometric (Fm3̄m), Mohs 2.5-3, specific gravity 10.5 (one of the heaviest native metals). The S-depletion mineral: native silver only forms where every sulfur atom is already claimed and the fluid is strongly reducing — the inverse of normal supersaturation logic. Famous wire-silver localities (Kongsberg Norway, Keweenaw Michigan, Cobalt Ontario) all sit in S-depleted reducing pockets. Tarnishes inevitably to a dark acanthite rind from atmospheric H₂S; freshly broken surfaces still flash with the brightest metallic luster known to mineralogy. Source: research/research-native-silver.md (boss commit f2939da), Boyle 1968 (GSA Bulletin 79), Bjørlykke 1959 (NGU 207).
native_silver.audit_status: Round 8a-3 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The first depletion-gate engine in the sim: native silver fires only when fluid.S < 2 ppm AND fluid.O2 < 0.3 — the geological signature of every Kongsberg-type wire-silver locality. Engine includes tarnish branch (when S returns to fluid > 5 ppm, the surface skins over with acanthite as a dissolution event). Habit variants: wire (low T + high σ, Kongsberg), dendritic (mid σ, Cobalt-Ontario), massive (low σ, Keweenaw), cubic_crystal (rare hypogene primary with {111} penetration twin). Activates the dormant Ag pool at Tsumeb supergene + Bisbee oxidation cap, where dissolving acanthite/tetrahedrite releases Ag⁺ that meets a deeper reducing pocket. Tarnish-clock mechanic (per-step acanthite-rind accumulation regardless of S availability) is deferred to 8a-3b alongside the same mechanic for native_arsenic / native_tellurium / native_bismuth. Sources: Boyle 1968 (GSA Bulletin 79), Bjørlykke 1959 (NGU 207), Kissin & Mango 2014 (CIM SV 54), boss research file research/research-native-silver.md.

native_arsenic.description: As — elemental arsenic, the residual-overflow native element. Trigonal (R3̄m), Mohs 3-4, perfect basal {0001} cleavage that splits like dark mica. Tin-white to steel-gray metallic on fresh fracture, tarnishes to arsenolite (As₂O₃) within hours. Forms only when every other As-consumer in the fluid (Fe → arsenopyrite, Ni → nickeline, Co → safflorite, S → realgar/orpiment) has had its share — the leftover phase. Famous Co-Ni-Ag vein districts (Freiberg Saxony, Sainte-Marie-aux-Mines Alsace, Příbram Czech) host native arsenic as the residual after the Co/Ni/Ag captured the metals and S was already locked in. Sources: research/research-native-arsenic.md (boss commit f2939da), Schiferl 1969 (Acta Cryst. B25), Petruk 1971 (Cobalt-Ag paragenesis).
native_arsenic.thermal_decomp_reaction: As (s) → As (vapor) — sublimes at 615°C and 1 atm without melting
native_arsenic.audit_status: Round 8b-1 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The residual-overflow native element: hard gates at S>10 (overflows to realgar/orpiment/arsenopyrite), Fe>50 (overflows to arsenopyrite), O₂>0.5 (overflows to arsenate suite). Habit variants: massive_granular (Freiberg ore form), reniform (Akatani botryoidal), rhombohedral_crystal (rare high-T crystals), arsenolamprite (Bi-rich variety). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch when O₂>0.7 (As goes back to fluid as AsO₄³⁻, feeds the supergene arsenate cascade: scorodite/erythrite/annabergite/olivenite/mimetite). Tarnish-clock for the As₂O₃ surface bloom is deferred to 8a-3b alongside other native-element tarnish mechanics. Sources: Schiferl 1969 (Acta Cryst. B25), Petruk 1971 (Cobalt-Ag paragenesis), boss research file research/research-native-arsenic.md.

native_sulfur.description: S — elemental sulfur, the bright canary-yellow native non-metal built from crown-shaped S₈ rings. Orthorhombic α-S (room T) or monoclinic β-S (T>95.5°C, unstable below). Mohs 1.5-2.5, fragile, thermally sensitive — a warm hand can crack a cool specimen. The synproportionation mineral: native S lives on the H₂S/SO₄²⁻ redox boundary where the reaction H₂S + SO₄²⁻ → 2S⁰ + H₂O drops elemental S out of solution. Forms in volcanic fumaroles (Vulcano, El Desierto), salt-dome caprocks via Desulfovibrio bacterial sulfate reduction (Tarnobrzeg), and Sicilian sulfur-vug evaporites with celestine companions (Agrigento — type locality, crystals to 20+ cm). Sources: research/research-native-sulfur.md (boss commit f2939da), Holland 1965 (Econ. Geol. 60).
native_sulfur.thermal_decomp_reaction: S (s) → S (l) — melts at 115.2°C; burns to SO₂ at ~250°C in air
native_sulfur.audit_status: Round 8b-2 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The synproportionation Eh-window engine: native S fires only when 0.1<O₂<0.7 (the H₂S/SO₄²⁻ boundary), pH<5 (H₂S dominant regime), and Fe+Cu+Pb+Zn<100 (base-metal sulfide capture gate). Habit variants: bipyramidal_alpha (T<95°C, Sicilian Agrigento), prismatic_beta (95-119°C, RARE — converts to α with cracking), sublimation_crust (high σ + T>60°C, fumarole habit). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch (O₂>0.9 returns S to fluid as SO₄²⁻) and per-step pH acidification (synproportionation releases minor H⁺). THERMAL_DECOMPOSITION at 115°C (melting point) — outside most scenario T ranges. Sources: Holland 1965 (Econ. Geol. 60), boss research file research/research-native-sulfur.md.

habit_variants.name: prismatic_hex

habit_variants.name: reticulated

native_tellurium.description: Te — elemental tellurium, the rarest of the residual-overflow native trio. Trigonal (P3₁21), Mohs 2-2.5, SG 6.2, perfect {1010} prismatic cleavage. Tin-white to steel-gray metallic, tarnishes to TeO₂ within days of exposure. Tellurium is rarer than platinum in Earth's crust; native Te only crystallizes when every telluride-forming metal (Au → calaverite/sylvanite, Ag → hessite, Pb → altaite, Bi → tetradymite, Hg → coloradoite) has had its fill — a relic of excess. Type localities: Cripple Creek (Colorado), Kalgoorlie (Western Australia, the richest Au-Te deposit on Earth), Emperor Mine (Vatukoula, Fiji). Sources: research/research-native-tellurium.md (boss commit f2939da), Spry & Thieben 1996 (Mineralium Deposita 31).
native_tellurium.thermal_decomp_reaction: Te (s) → Te (l) — melts at 449.5°C; boils at 988°C
native_tellurium.audit_status: Round 8b-3 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The metal-telluride-overflow engine: hard gates at Au>1 (overflows to calaverite/sylvanite), Ag>5 (overflows to hessite), O₂>0.5 (overflows to tellurite/tellurate). Soft suppressors for Pb (altaite) and Bi (tetradymite). Hg not currently tracked in FluidChemistry; coloradoite (HgTe) gate deferred to a future Hg-plumbing round. Habit variants: prismatic_hex (high-T striated prisms, the Kalgoorlie museum habit), granular (Cripple Creek ore form), reticulated (low-T filiform). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch. THERMAL_DECOMPOSITION at 449°C melting point. Currently absent_at_seed_42: porphyry has Au=2 (above gate of >1) and Ag=8 (above gate of >5), so Te stays in solution destined for calaverite/sylvanite/hessite — those engines don't exist yet. The Au-Te coupling round (calaverite + sylvanite + hessite + altaite + tetradymite + coloradoite) is now unblocked — telluride engines are the natural Round 9 lift; once Au is locked in calaverite, residual Te will be free to fire as native. Sources: Spry & Thieben 1996 (Mineralium Deposita 31), boss research file research/research-native-tellurium.md.

nickeline.description: NiAs — hexagonal nickel arsenide, the namesake of the NiAs structure-type. Pale copper-red metallic on fresh fracture, the diagnostic color that fooled early miners into mistaking it for copper ore (the 'kupfernickel' = copper-devil etymology). Mohs 5-5.5, SG 7.8. Forms in high-T reducing veins where Ni and As are present together; cooler / As-poor regimes push the chemistry to millerite (NiS) or annabergite (the supergene Ni arsenate). Type-locality habit at Cobalt, Ontario; also documented at Schneeberg (Saxony), Příbram (Czech), and Tunaberg (Sweden). Sources: research/research-nickeline.md (boss commit f2939da), Petruk 1971.
nickeline.audit_status: Round 8c-2 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. Ni+As required + reducing (O₂<0.6) + T 200-500°C with peak 300-450. Substrate preference: arsenopyrite (the paragenetic predecessor). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch that releases Ni²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ to fluid, feeding the supergene annabergite cascade. Activates with Round 8c-1 Bisbee Ni=70 + As=8 chemistry; expected to fire in seed-42 Bisbee primary stages and supergene_oxidation supergene cascade. Mutual-priority dispatcher with millerite: when As>30 + T>200, nickeline takes priority (NiAs more stable than NiS at high-T As-saturation). Sources: Petruk 1971 (Co-Ni-Ag paragenesis), boss research file research/research-nickeline.md.

millerite.description: NiS — trigonal nickel sulfide, brass-yellow to bronze-yellow metallic. Mohs 3-3.5, the rare nickel mineral that grows as hair-thin acicular needles in geode cavities. Forms in lower-T regimes than nickeline (NiAs); when As is depleted and Ni + S remain, NiS takes the field. The capillary habit is so distinctive that 'millerite' is shorthand among collectors for any radiating-needle sulfide. Showcase localities: Halls Gap (Kentucky), Sterling Hill (NJ), Glamorgan (Wales). Sources: research/research-millerite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bayliss 1969 (Geochim. Cosmochim. Acta 33).
millerite.audit_status: Round 8c-3 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. Ni+S required + reducing (O₂<0.6) + T 100-400°C with peak 200-350. Mutual-exclusion gate with nickeline: when As>30 AND T>200, returns 0 (nickeline takes priority — NiAs more stable than NiS at high-T As-saturation). The capillary habit (hair-fine brass-yellow needles in radiating sprays) is the diagnostic morphology — Halls Gap KY, Sterling Hill NJ, Glamorgan Wales produce showcase specimens. Substrate preference: pyrite (the geological companion). Activates with Round 8c-1 Bisbee Ni=70 chemistry; expected to fire in supergene_oxidation when As is depleted from earlier scorodite/erythrite/annabergite. Sources: Bayliss 1969 (NiS-NiAs equilibria), boss research file research/research-millerite.md.

cobaltite.description: CoAsS — orthorhombic cobalt sulfarsenide (pseudo-cubic, pyritohedral habit). Tin-white with diagnostic pinkish blush from surface Co oxidation, Mohs 5.5. The cobalt analog of arsenopyrite. The three-element gate (Co + As + S all required simultaneously) makes this rare; the Cobalt-Ontario type locality is one of the few places it forms in well-developed crystals. Indian jewellers used powdered cobaltite ('sehta') to make blue enamel on gold ornaments — the source of the first Co ever isolated from a non-arsenide mineral. Sources: research/research-cobaltite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bayliss 1968 (Mineral. Mag. 36, on cobaltite-arsenopyrite substitution).
cobaltite.audit_status: Round 8c-4 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. THREE-ELEMENT GATE engine: Co + As + S all required simultaneously at minimum thresholds (Co≥50, As≥100, S≥50). First three-element gate in the sim. Reducing (O₂<0.5) + T 300-600°C with peak 400-500. Substrate preference: arsenopyrite (the paragenetic predecessor). Habit variants: massive_granular (low σ, ore form), pyritohedral (mid σ, {210} striated faces — Cobalt Ontario diagnostic), reniform (high σ, botryoidal). Glaucodot series captured in narrator: Fe>100 in fluid yields '(Co,Fe)AsS' note — the iron-rich end of the cobaltite-glaucodot solid solution (Skutterud type). Engine includes oxidative-dissolution branch: when O₂>0.7, releases Co²⁺ + AsO₄³⁻ + SO₄²⁻ to fluid, feeding the existing erythrite engine downstream — this is THE primary source for the magenta-pink 'cobalt bloom' supergene cascade. Activates with Round 8c-1 Bisbee Co=80 + As=8 + S=150 chemistry; expected to fire in seed-42 Bisbee primary stage. Sources: Bayliss 1968 (cobaltite-arsenopyrite substitution), boss research file research/research-cobaltite.md.

descloizite.description: Pb(Zn,Cu)VO₄(OH) — orthorhombic Pb-Zn vanadate, the Zn end of the descloizite-mottramite series. Cherry-red to brown-red, Mohs 3-3.5; the Tsumeb display standard. Forms in supergene oxidation zones where Pb-Zn sulfide ore (galena + sphalerite) has weathered AND V is delivered by groundwater (red-bed roll-front signature). Cu/Zn ratio dispatch: when Cu > Zn, mottramite (the olive-green Cu sibling) takes priority instead. Sources: research/research-descloizite.md (boss commit f2939da), Strunz 1959 (Tsumeb monograph).
descloizite.audit_status: Round 8d-1a (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html. Fires at seed-42 in supergene_oxidation when Tsumeb's Pb=60 + Zn=90 + V=1.5+events meet the gates. Source: Strunz 1959, boss research file.

mottramite.description: Pb(Cu,Zn)VO₄(OH) — orthorhombic Pb-Cu vanadate, the Cu end of the descloizite-mottramite series. Olive-green to yellowish-green from the Cu chromophore (distinguishing it at a glance from cherry-red descloizite), Mohs 3-3.5. Type locality: Mottram St. Andrew, Cheshire (England), 1876. Tsumeb produces the museum-grade specimens. Cu/Zn ratio dispatch: when Zn ≥ Cu, descloizite takes priority. Sources: research/research-mottramite.md (boss commit f2939da).

raspite.description: PbWO₄ — monoclinic lead tungstate, RARE. Same composition as stolzite but a different crystal system; stolzite (tetragonal) is favored ~90% of the time when both can form (kinetic-preference dispatcher in check_nucleation reflects natural rarity). Honey-yellow to brownish-yellow tabular crystals, Mohs 2.5-3, perfect {100} cleavage. Type locality: Broken Hill, NSW, Australia. Sources: research/research-raspite.md (boss commit f2939da).
raspite.audit_status: Round 8d-2a (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html. Same Pb+W+oxidizing chemistry as stolzite but narrower T window (10-50°C, peak 20-40); sigma threshold 1.4 (vs stolzite's 1.0) reflects rarity. Kinetic-preference dispatcher in check_nucleation: when both raspite + stolzite gates clear, stolzite wins ~90% of rolls (random.random() < 0.9 zeros raspite's sigma). Tsumeb scenario W=20 plumbing activates the engine. Source: boss research file.

stolzite.description: PbWO₄ — tetragonal lead tungstate, the lead analog of scheelite (CaWO₄). Honey-yellow to orange-yellow Mohs 2.5-3, the more common of the two PbWO₄ polymorphs (~90% of natural occurrences vs raspite's monoclinic ~10%). Type locality: Cínovec (Czech Republic). Broken Hill (Australia) and Tsumeb (Namibia) produce the museum specimens — sharp honey-yellow dipyramids. Sources: research/research-stolzite.md (boss commit f2939da).

olivenite.description: Cu₂AsO₄(OH) — orthorhombic Cu arsenate, the Cu end of the olivenite-adamite series. Olive-green to grayish-green (the Cu chromophore — olive in name and color), Mohs 3-4. Forms in Cu-rich supergene oxidation zones; Cornwall is the type locality, with Tsumeb and Bisbee producing showcase modern specimens. Cu/Zn ratio dispatch: when Zn > Cu, adamite (the Zn-end-member of the same arsenate structure) takes priority. Sources: research/research-olivenite.md (boss commit f2939da).
olivenite.audit_status: Round 8d-3 (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html. chalcanthite.description: CuSO₄·5H₂O — the bright sky-blue water-soluble Cu sulfate, the [Cu(H₂O)₅]²⁺ chromophore one of the most intensely-colored minerals. Triclinic (P1̄), Mohs 2.5, perfect {110} cleavage. The most water-soluble mineral in the sim — re-dissolves when fluid salinity drops below 4 OR pH rises above 5 (per-step hook in VugSimulator.run_step). Geologically authentic: every chalcanthite specimen is a temporary victory over entropy. The terminal phase of the Cu-sulfate oxidation cascade (chalcopyrite → bornite → chalcocite → covellite → cuprite → brochantite → antlerite → chalcanthite). Forms in arid strongly-oxidizing very-acidic salt-concentrated drainage: Chuquicamata mine walls (Chile), Rio Tinto AMD seeps (Spain), Atacama desert evaporite crusts. Most 'natural' specimens on the market are lab-grown. Sources: research/research-chalcanthite.md (boss commit f2939da), Bandy 1938 (Am. Mineral. 23).
chalcanthite.audit_status: Round 8e (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + web/index.html; JSON entry mirrored to docs/. The water-solubility metastability mechanic: per-step hook in VugSimulator.run_step re-dissolves chalcanthite crystals when fluid.salinity < 4 OR fluid.pH > 5. Distinct from THERMAL_DECOMPOSITION (high-T destruction) and PARAMORPH_TRANSITIONS (in-place mineral change) — this is just chemistry, the geological truth that every chalcanthite is a temporary victory over entropy. Habit variants: stalactitic (high σ, the Chuquicamata mine-wall drip habit), tabular (RARE prismatic crystals), efflorescent_crust (low σ powdery bloom). Salinity gate: salinity < 6 → 0 (needs concentrated drainage to overcome ~20 g/100mL solubility). pH gate: pH > 4 → 0 (the most acid-loving Cu sulfate). Source: boss research file research-chalcanthite.md.

rosasite.description: (Cu,Zn)₂(CO₃)(OH)₂ — monoclinic supergene carbonate, the Cu-dominant member of the rosasite-aurichalcite pair. Velvety blue-green botryoidal spheres on weathered Cu-Zn sulfide deposits; the diagnostic habit is sky-blue spherical aggregates on red limonite (Mapimi, Mexico). Forms when chalcopyrite + sphalerite weather together at the supergene front, releasing Cu²⁺ + Zn²⁺ + CO₃²⁻ into shallow groundwater that precipitates the carbonate at near-neutral pH. The Cu:Zn ratio in the parent fluid is the key control: rosasite forms when Cu/(Cu+Zn) > 0.5; aurichalcite forms when the ratio inverts — same broth, different outcome. Mohs 4, blue-green streak, splintery fracture. Type locality: Rosas Mine, Sardinia (1908). Source: Pinch & Wilson 1977 (Tsumeb monograph); research/research-rosasite.md.

rosasite.audit_status: Round 9a (Apr 2026). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + index.html. Mirror of aurichalcite — same parent fluid, opposite outcome based on ratio. Habit variants: botryoidal (default, the diagnostic Mapimi habit), acicular_radiating (low T + high σ delicate sprays), encrusting (low σ thin crust). Sources: research/research-rosasite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Pinch & Wilson 1977 (Tsumeb monograph).

aurichalcite.description: (Zn,Cu)₅(CO₃)₂(OH)₆ — monoclinic supergene carbonate, the Zn-dominant mirror of rosasite. Delicate pale blue-green tufted divergent sprays — high birefringence makes them shimmer under cross-polarized light. Forms in the same supergene parent fluid as rosasite (Cu + Zn + CO₃ + O₂) but precipitates only when Zn/(Cu+Zn) > 0.5; the two species are typically intergrown, separated only by ratio. Mohs 2 (scratches with a fingernail), light blue streak. Named after Plato's mythical orichalcum — the lost gold-alloy of Atlantis. Type locality: Loktevskoye Mine, Western Siberia (1839); the most aesthetic specimens come from Mapimi, Mexico. Source: research/research-aurichalcite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a).

torbernite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·12H₂O — tetragonal uranyl phosphate, the P-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with zeunerite for AsO₄³⁻ and carnotite for VO₄³⁻ the other branches). Forms emerald-green to apple-green thin tabular plates that look like green mica; strongly radioactive (U⁶⁺ in the lattice), notably non-fluorescent (Cu²⁺ quenches uranyl emission). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers to mobile U⁶⁺ and meets phosphate-bearing groundwater alongside Cu²⁺ — torbernite wins the cation race when P/(P+As) > 0.5 in the local fluid. Mohs 2-2.5, dehydrates irreversibly to metatorbernite (8 H₂O) above ~75°C. Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (Ore Mountains). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977 monographs; research/research-torbernite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a).

acid_dissolution.reaction: Cu(UO₂)₂(PO₄)₂·12H₂O + 6H⁺ → Cu²⁺ + 2(UO₂)²⁺ + 2H₃PO₄ + 12H₂O (dissolves readily in mineral acids)

torbernite.audit_status: Round 9b (Apr 2026), updated 9c (Apr 2026): denominator widened from P+As to P+As+V so V-rich fluid now properly blocks torbernite (carnotite gets it instead). First mineral with the anion-competition mechanic — three-way fork over the U⁶⁺ cation between PO₄³⁻ (torbernite), AsO₄³⁻ (zeunerite, also 9b), and VO₄³⁻ (carnotite, 9c). supersaturation_torbernite returns 0 when P/(P+As+V) < 0.5. Habit variants: tabular_plates (default Schneeberg), micaceous_book (high σ stacks), encrusting (low σ powdery crust). Source: research/research-torbernite.md.

zeunerite.description: Cu(UO₂)₂(AsO₄)₂·(10-16)H₂O — tetragonal uranyl arsenate, the As-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio. Visually almost indistinguishable from torbernite — same emerald-green color, same square tabular habit, same micaceous cleavage — distinguishable only by chemistry. The arsenic is the giveaway: zeunerite localities are almost always former mining districts with arsenic-bearing primary ores (arsenopyrite, tennantite). Forms at the supergene oxidation front when uraninite weathers alongside As-rich primary sulfides; zeunerite wins the anion race when As/(P+As) > 0.5. Mohs 2.5; dehydrates irreversibly to metazeunerite (8 H₂O) above ~75°C; non-fluorescent (Cu²⁺ quenches). Type locality: Schneeberg, Saxony (1872). Sources: Pinch & Wilson 1977; research/research-zeunerite.md.
zeunerite.audit_status: Round 9b (Apr 2026), updated 9c (Apr 2026): denominator widened from P+As to P+As+V so V-rich fluid now properly blocks zeunerite (carnotite gets it instead). Engine + supersaturation + nucleation + narrator complete in vugg.py + index.html. supersaturation_zeunerite returns 0 when As/(P+As+V) < 0.5 — the As-dominant branch of the now-complete 3-way anion-competition trio (torbernite/zeunerite/carnotite). Isostructural with torbernite (Schneeberg pair); shares all habit-selection logic. Source: research/research-zeunerite.md.

habit_variants.name: earthy_crust
habit_variants.trigger: default — canary-yellow earthy stain, the diagnostic Colorado Plateau habit. One percent of carnotite stains an entire sandstone outcrop the color of school buses.

habit_variants.name: tabular_plates

habit_variants.trigger: low σ — powdery yellow disseminations, often around organic carbon (petrified wood).

carnotite.description: K₂(UO₂)₂(VO₄)₂·3H₂O — monoclinic uranyl vanadate, the V-branch of the autunite-group anion-competition trio (with torbernite for phosphate and zeunerite for arsenate). Bright canary-yellow earthy crusts on sandstone — the mineral that paints the desert. One percent of carnotite by weight is enough to stain an entire Jurassic outcrop the color of school buses, which is how the Colorado Plateau uranium districts were prospected before instruments. Formed where oxidizing groundwater carries U⁶⁺ + V⁵⁺ + K⁺ together at the supergene front, often concentrated around organic reducing traps (petrified wood, carbonaceous shales). Mohs ~2 (soft, earthy), strongly radioactive, non-fluorescent (vanadate matrix quenches the uranyl emission). Type locality: Roc Creek, Montrose County, Colorado (1899). Sources: research/research-carnotite.md (boss commit 3bfdf4a); Colorado Plateau uranium-vanadium roll-front geology.

carnotite.audit_status: Round 9c (Apr 2026). Singleton — V-branch of the uranyl anion-competition trio (with torbernite for P-branch, zeunerite for As-branch, both shipped in 9b). Different cation (K instead of Cu), different crystal system (monoclinic instead of tetragonal), different habit (canary-yellow earthy crusts instead of emerald-green tabular plates), same anion-competition mechanic. Completes the 3-branch generalization: 9c also widens torbernite + zeunerite supersat denominators from P+As to P+As+V so V-rich fluid properly routes to carnotite. No current scenario has the K+U+V+low-T+oxidizing conjunction (rad_peg has K+U but is hot+reducing+V=0; bisbee has K but no U or V; supergene_oxidation has K+V but no U) — same content gap as 9b's torbernite/zeunerite. Engine + test_cases verified. Sources: research/research-carnotite.md; Colorado Plateau roll-front geology; Roc Creek type locality (1899).

test_cases.name: v_dominant_fires
test_cases.rationale: V/(P+As+V)=0.80 — anion gate passes for the V-branch. K+U+V all above ingredient thresholds; T in window; oxidizing; near-neutral pH (V is mobile as VO₄³⁻ above pH 6).

test_cases.name: p_dominant_blocks
test_cases.rationale: P-dominant fluid — torbernite wins, carnotite ratio-blocked.

test_cases.name: as_dominant_blocks
test_cases.rationale: As-dominant fluid — zeunerite wins, carnotite ratio-blocked.

test_cases.name: no_potassium_blocks
test_cases.rationale: K=0 fails K>=5 ingredient gate. Carnotite is K-specific; without K it would form tyuyamunite (Ca analogue) instead — variant deferred to a future round.

test_cases.name: no_uranium_blocks
test_cases.rationale: U=0 fails U>=0.3 ingredient gate. No uranyl, no uranyl mineral.

test_cases.name: no_vanadium_blocks
test_cases.rationale: V=0 fails V>=1.0 ingredient gate. Without vanadate the only uranyl path is torbernite/zeunerite (which also fail here since P=As=0).

test_cases.name: reducing_blocks
test_cases.rationale: Reducing fluid keeps U as insoluble U⁴⁺ (uraninite). Carnotite needs U⁶⁺.

test_cases.name: too_hot_blocks
test_cases.rationale: T=80°C above 50°C ceiling — carnotite dehydrates above ~100°C, structure collapses; this gate blocks before that.

test_cases.name: acidic_blocks
test_cases.rationale: pH=4.0 below the 5.0-7.5 stability window. Below pH 6, vanadate is no longer the dominant V species — VO₂⁺ takes over and carnotite chemistry breaks.
